turns.student: What brings you in today?
turns.patient: I've been having some really bad abdominal pain and bleeding from my rectum for the past three weeks. It's just getting worse and worse, and I'm really worried about it.

turns.student: Can you tell me more about what you've been feeling?
turns.patient: The pain is crampy and mostly on the left side, but it's like a burning sensation too. Sometimes it feels like it's going to burst or something, which is really scary. And the bleeding... it's just blood mixed in with my stool, not just a little bit either - it's pretty substantial.

turns.student: When you have those episodes, do you notice anything else happening to your body?
turns.patient: Yeah, I've been having diarrhea a lot too. Like, 8-10 times a day, it's really frequent and loose. It's also waking me up at night sometimes, which is no fun. And I feel pretty tired all the time, my work performance has been suffering because of it.

turns.student: Any sweating or clamminess when the symptoms hit?
turns.patient: No, I don't really experience any sweating or clamminess. To be honest, I just feel a general sense of fatigue and weakness, like I'm running on empty all the time.

turns.student: So no perspiration at all during the episodes?
turns.patient: No, not really. Like I said, I just feel kind of drained and weak, it's more of a mental exhaustion than anything physical.

turns.student: Does the discomfort stay in one spot, or does it move around?
turns.patient: The pain is usually on the left side, but sometimes it feels like it's radiating down to my thighs. It can be a bit unpredictable, but when it's bad, it's like a constant ache that won't go away.

turns.student: Does it radiate anywhere — like your arms, neck, or jaw?
turns.patient: It usually stays in my lower abdomen and pelvis area, but sometimes it can feel like it's moving up into my back. I've had some achy pain in my knees and ankles too, but that's not constant and seems to be unrelated to the stomach stuff.

turns.student: Any tingling or numbness in your arms when it happens?
turns.patient: No, no tingling or numbness in my arms. Like I said, most of the discomfort is limited to my lower abdomen and pelvis area, with some occasional radiation up into my back. The joint pain in my knees and ankles feels like a separate issue from my stomach problems.

turns.student: What about your extremities — any tingling, pain, or unusual sensations?
turns.patient: No, nothing out of the ordinary with my hands or feet. Like I said, the discomfort is mostly confined to my lower abdomen and pelvis area, with some occasional radiation up into my back. The joint pain in my knees and ankles feels like a separate issue from my stomach problems, it's more of a dull ache that comes and goes.

turns.student: Have you noticed any swelling anywhere? Ankles, feet, hands?
turns.patient: No, I don't have any swelling in my ankles or feet. Like I said, the joint pain is just a dull ache, it's not accompanied by any swelling or redness. It's really more of a nuisance than anything else, to be honest.

turns.student: Do your shoes feel tighter than usual, or any puffiness in your legs?
turns.patient: No, my shoes don't feel tighter and I haven't noticed any puffiness in my legs. Like I said, it's just a dull ache in my knees and ankles that comes and goes, it's not really affecting anything except maybe making me more tired.

turns.student: Any stomach issues during the episodes? Nausea, queasiness, upset stomach?
turns.patient: Actually, yeah... I've been feeling pretty nauseous sometimes when the diarrhea hits. It's like my stomach is just churning and it's hard to keep food down. But it's not always there, maybe 50% of the time or so. And even when I do eat, it's usually just takeout or something easy because cooking seems too much effort when I'm feeling this way.

turns.student: Do you feel sick to your stomach when the symptoms come on?
turns.patient: Yeah, that's right. Sometimes I'll feel really queasy and nauseous when the diarrhea hits. It's like my stomach is just churning and it's hard to keep food down. And even when I do eat, it's usually just something easy because cooking seems too much effort when I'm feeling this way.

turns.student: Have you had any fevers, chills, or night sweats?
turns.patient: To be honest, I've had a low-grade fever a few times a week. It's not like a full-blown fever, but more of a slight elevation in temperature, usually around 98-99°F. And sometimes I'll feel a bit clammy or sweaty, but it's not anything that would really wake me up or make me think I'm seriously ill. It's just kind of... there.

turns.student: Have you noticed any changes in your appetite or weight?
turns.patient: To be honest, my appetite has been pretty bad lately. I've been eating more takeout and convenience food because it's just easier when I don't feel like cooking, but even that doesn't always sound good to me. As for weight loss, I've lost about 9 pounds over the past three weeks, which is a lot for me since I'm normally pretty active and eat regular meals.

turns.student: Tell me about your lifestyle — diet, exercise, daily routine.
turns.patient:
Well, like I said, my diet's been pretty irregular lately. I've been eating a lot of takeout and convenience food because it's just easier when I don't feel like cooking. And to be honest, even that doesn't always sound good to me, which is really weird for me since I'm normally a big eater. As for exercise, I used to be pretty active, but lately, my symptoms have been so bad that I've had to cut back on working out and just try to get through the day without too much discomfort.

I work as a software engineer at a startup, and it's been really stressful lately with long hours and high expectations. I'm usually up late into the night trying to meet deadlines or fix bugs, which doesn't help my symptoms at all. And when I do have some downtime, I've actually started drinking more beer than usual just to try and relax and take my mind off things.

turns.student: How active are you physically? Do you work out or walk regularly?
turns.patient: I used to be pretty active, I'd go to the gym 3-4 times a week, but since this started, I've had to cut back significantly. Lately, it's been more like once every 10 days if that. Even just going for a short walk feels like too much when my stomach is acting up.

turns.student: What does a typical day of eating look like for you?
turns.patient: Honestly, it's been pretty irregular lately. I've been grabbing takeout or ordering food delivery because cooking seems like too much effort when I'm feeling this way. Sometimes I'll eat a sandwich or a salad if that's all I feel like eating. I used to be a big breakfast eater, but even that's been hard for me to get into the habit of lately. Most days it's just whatever is easy and convenient, which isn't great for my diet, I know.

turns.student: Any medical problems in your family? Parents, siblings?
turns.patient: My older sister was diagnosed with Crohn's disease when she was 30 years old. That was a few years ago, but other than that, I don't have any known medical conditions in my family. My parents are both relatively healthy, my mom is fine and my dad has type 2 diabetes, but he's been managing it for a while now.

turns.student: Specifically, has anyone in your family had serious diseases?
turns.patient: My older sister was diagnosed with Crohn's disease when she was 30 years old. That's been a few years ago now, and I've been keeping an eye out for any similar symptoms in myself, but so far, nothing like that has shown up... until recently, anyway.
